title: US-Iran War Ceasefire Negotiations
description: Tracking the diplomacy around the 2026 US-Iran war that began with US strikes alongside Israel and rattled global energy markets. Pakistan and China are acting as de facto mediators while the White House pushes for a one-page memorandum of understanding covering nuclear enrichment, sanctions, and Strait of Hormuz transit.

timeline.title: First US-Iran Two-Week Ceasefire, Mediated by Pakistan
timeline.summary: US and Iran agreed to a 14-day ceasefire mediated by Pakistan, with the New York Times reporting China played a quiet but crucial role in pushing Tehran to the table. Trump credited Beijing publicly while threatening 50% secondary tariffs on any country supplying Iran with weapons.

timeline.title: Iran Submits New Peace Proposal — White House Response Pending
timeline.summary: Iran tabled a fresh proposal centered on phased sanctions relief tied to nuclear monitoring concessions. Iran's Foreign Ministry confirmed receipt of a US response in early May, with Pakistan continuing to relay messages between the two governments.

timeline.title: Wang Yi Hosts Iran's Araghchi in Beijing — China Pushes 'Comprehensive Ceasefire'
timeline.summary: Chinese FM Wang Yi met Iranian FM Abbas Araghchi in Beijing and publicly called for a comprehensive ceasefire and the immediate reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. The meeting positioned Beijing as a parallel mediator just one week before the May 14–15 Trump-Xi summit, where Iran is now expected to share the agenda with Taiwan and trade.

timeline.title: Axios: US-Iran Closing on One-Page MOU to End the War
timeline.summary: The framework would have Iran commit to a moratorium on nuclear enrichment in exchange for US sanctions relief, the release of frozen Iranian funds, and a mutual reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, followed by a 30-day window for a fuller deal. The White House expected Iranian responses on key points within 48 hours. Trump simultaneously warned Iran would be bombed at a 'much higher level' if no agreement materialized.

timeline.title: Iran 'Strongly Rejects' Parts of MOU; US Strikes Bandar Abbas and Qeshm; Saudi/Kuwait Lift Base Restrictions
timeline.summary: Iran's Foreign Ministry confirmed it conveyed its response to the 14-point MOU but said it 'strongly rejected' some terms — most notably the demand that Tehran ship its enriched uranium out of the country and accept a permanent enrichment cap. In parallel, US Central Command struck Iran's port city of Bandar Abbas and the Strait of Hormuz island of Qeshm. Saudi Arabia and Kuwait lifted the base and airspace restrictions imposed during Project Freedom, leaving the US the option to resume the escort operation. Trump publicly said the war 'will be over quickly' but warned of bombing at a 'much higher level' if Tehran refuses.

timeline.title: Israel Strikes Beirut for First Time Since April Ceasefire — Re-Opens Lebanon Front Around Iran Talks
timeline.summary: Israel hit the southern suburbs of Beirut for the first time since the April 16 US-brokered ceasefire, killing a senior Hezbollah Radwan-Force commander, and continued strikes across southern Lebanon (Ain Baal, Dibbin, Nabatieh). Tehran has consistently insisted any wider peace deal must include Israel halting its fight with Hezbollah — making the Beirut strike a direct complication for the MOU negotiations.

timeline.title: Fox News / NCRI Reveal 'Rainbow Site' — Alleged Secret 2,500-Acre Tritium Facility Threatens MOU Talks
timeline.summary: Fox News aired NCRI satellite images of an alleged 2,500-acre secret nuclear weapons facility ('Rainbow Site') in Semnan Province, Iran, reportedly dedicated to tritium extraction for nuclear warhead enhancement. Iran's UN mission called the report fabricated; FM Araghchi blamed Israel. The revelation, if verified, would undermine Iran's peaceful-use claims and could collapse the 14-point MOU framework. US strikes on Bandar Abbas/Qeshm remain the latest US military action.

timeline.title: MOU Talks Frozen — Iran Rejected Key Clauses, Rainbow Site Unaddressed
timeline.summary: As of May 10, no MOU has been signed. Iran reportedly rejected key clauses including the requirement to ship enriched uranium abroad and the timeline on the enrichment moratorium (US 20 years vs Iran 5 years, with 12-15 years as current compromise). The Rainbow Site tritium-facility revelation (Fox News/NCRI, May 9) has not been addressed by either side and complicates the inspection provisions of the draft agreement. Indirect talks via the Omani channel remain the active diplomatic track.

timeline.title: Fourth Round of Indirect Oman Talks — Tehran Still Reviewing 14-Point US Proposal Ahead of Trump's Middle East Trip
timeline.summary: Iranian and US delegations met today via Omani mediation for the fourth round of indirect MOU talks, scheduled ahead of Trump's Middle East travel later this week. Iran's MFA spokesperson Esmaeil Baghaei said Tehran is still 'reviewing' the 14-point US proposal and considering its response. Sticking points include the duration of the enrichment moratorium, sequencing of the Strait of Hormuz reopening (Iran wants Hormuz first, nuclear later), and the unaddressed Rainbow Site tritium-facility allegations. The May 7 Strait of Hormuz fire exchange has not formally ended the April 8 ceasefire; Trump called the strikes 'just a love tap.'

timeline.title: Trump Rejects Iran's Counterproposal — Ceasefire 'On Massive Life Support,' Hormuz Closed 10 Weeks
timeline.summary: Iran sent its formal response to the US 14-point MOU via Pakistani mediation; Trump immediately called it 'totally unacceptable.' Tehran demanded war reparations, full Hormuz sovereignty, complete sanctions removal, and release of frozen assets — all beyond the US framework. Iran vowed to 'never bow.' New Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei issued undisclosed 'decisive military directives.' The Strait of Hormuz has been closed for ten consecutive weeks; Saudi Aramco publicly warned the disruption may not resolve in 2026. The US separately sanctioned 12 companies for facilitating Iran-China oil trade. Global food prices rose for a third consecutive month (UN FAO) tied to the energy supply shock.

timeline.title: Trump Aides Say Resumption of Combat Now Seriously Under Consideration; CENTCOM Has Turned Around 58 Commercial Vessels at Hormuz
timeline.summary: Per NBC News and CBS reporting, Trump aides confirmed the president is now seriously considering resumption of major combat operations against Iran. Trump publicly repeated Iran's response was 'totally unacceptable' and 'stupid' and said he is impatient with the continued closure of the Strait of Hormuz. US CENTCOM disclosed it has now turned around 58 commercial vessels attempting to enter or exit Iranian ports through the Strait. Both sides have continued exchanging fire in and around Hormuz since the April 8 ceasefire technically took effect. Pakistan-mediated talks remain alive but with no movement on core sticking points (Hormuz sequencing, nuclear/ballistic constraints, sanctions). The Iran file is now the dominant agenda item for the Trump-Xi Beijing summit (May 13–15).

timeline.title: Iran File Now Center of Trump-Xi Bilateral; Regional Source: No MOU Movement Until After Beijing Summit
timeline.summary: Reporting from Al Jazeera and CNBC confirms the Iran file is now the centerpiece of Trump's bilateral with Xi in Beijing, with Trump pushing China to curtail purchases of Iranian crude and to refrain from any arms transfers. A regional source told Al Jazeera that 'talks are unlikely to make significant progress until Trump meets his Chinese counterpart Xi Jinping this week' — Beijing is the leverage point on Iranian oil buyers. The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed for a 10th week; crude is back above $100/bbl and US gasoline at $4.48/gal nationally. UK House of Commons briefing called full reopening 'a central and contentious issue' with no near-term path.

timeline.title: Trump-Xi Joint Pledge: Iran Must Not Get Nukes, Hormuz Must Stay Open, China to Withhold Military Equipment — But Ceasefire Survives Without Breakthrough
timeline.summary: As the Trump-Xi state visit concludes, the joint outcomes include explicit alignment on Iran: both leaders agreed Tehran must not obtain a nuclear weapon, that the Strait of Hormuz must remain open, and Xi pledged China will not supply military equipment to Iran. The pledges shape the diplomatic terrain but do not unlock the negotiation: Pakistan-mediated talks continue with Iran's phased proposal (Hormuz first, nuclear later) still rejected by Washington. The Strait remains effectively closed for a 10th consecutive week with almost no commercial shipping. Crude continues to trade above $100/bbl, US gasoline near $4.48/gal. No movement on the 14-point MOU. The ceasefire stays officially in effect but Trump's 'massive life support' framing is intact.

timeline.title: Hormuz Re-Escalates Within 24 Hours of Trump-Xi Pledge: Tanker Seized 38nm NE of Fujairah, Indian-Flagged Haji Ali Sunk Off Oman
timeline.summary: One day after Trump and Xi jointly committed in Beijing that the Strait of Hormuz must remain open, two maritime incidents tested the framework. UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) and the British military reported that an unnamed vessel anchored 38 nautical miles northeast of Fujairah was seized by unauthorized personnel and is now being redirected toward Iranian waters; investigation is ongoing. Separately, the Indian-flagged livestock carrier Haji Ali — en route from Somalia to Sharjah — sank off the Omani coast after an attack triggered an onboard fire; all 14 crew were rescued by the Omani Coast Guard. No party has formally claimed responsibility, but a senior Iranian official reiterated Tehran's claim of control over the waterway hours before the incidents. The Strait remains effectively closed for the 10th+ consecutive week. Brent crude settled near $111/bbl; WTI rose more than 3% Friday to above $104. The 14-point MOU remains frozen; Pakistan-mediated talks continue with Iran's phased proposal (Hormuz first, nuclear later) still rejected by Washington.
